html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Eleven — Part Two

The Book of Shofetim

SHOFETIM

The Book of Judges

Chapters 8 through 14

Gid'on — Avimelekh — Yiftakh — Shimshon Begins

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QJudga (4Q49) — Cave 4 — Primary DSS witness for Shofetim

4QJudgb (4Q50) — Cave 4 — Supporting DSS fragments

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Shofetim preserved in full

YHWH and The Creator only — no substitute titles

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHOFETIM — CHAPTERS 8 THROUGH 14

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 8 Gid'on Pursues Zevakh and Tzalmunna — The Ephod at Ofrah — Gid'on Dies

1 Then the men of Efrayim said to him, What is this that you have done to us, not to call us when you went to fight against Midyan? And they accused him fiercely.

2 And he said to them, What have I done now in comparison with you? Is not the gleaning of the grapes of Efrayim better than the grape harvest of Aviezer?

3 Creator has given into your hands the princes of Midyan, Orev and Ze'ev. What have I been able to do in comparison with you? Then their anger against him subsided when he said this.

4 And Gid'on came to the Yarden and crossed over, he and the three hundred men who were with him, exhausted yet pursuing.

5 So he said to the men of Sukkot, Please give loaves of bread to the people who follow me, for they are exhausted, and I am pursuing after Zevakh and Tzalmunna, the kings of Midyan.

6 And the officials of Sukkot said, Are the hands of Zevakh and Tzalmunna already in your hand, that we should give bread to your army?

7 So Gid'on said, Well then, when YHWH has given Zevakh and Tzalmunna into my hand, I will flail your flesh with the thorns of the wilderness and with briers.

8 And from there he went up to Penu'el, and spoke to them in the same way, and the men of Penu'el answered him as the men of Sukkot had answered.

9 And he said to the men of Penu'el, When I come again in peace, I will break down this tower.

10 Now Zevakh and Tzalmunna were in Karkor with their army, about fifteen thousand men, all who were left of all the army of the people of the East, for there had fallen one hundred and twenty thousand men who drew the sword.

11 And Gid'on went up by the way of the tent dwellers east of Novakh and Yogbehah and attacked the army, for the army felt secure.

12 And Zevakh and Tzalmunna fled, and he pursued them and captured the two kings of Midyan, Zevakh and Tzalmunna, and he threw all the army into a panic.

13 Then Gid'on the son of Yo'ash returned from the battle by the ascent of Kheres.

14 And he captured a young man of Sukkot and questioned him. And he wrote down for him the officials and elders of Sukkot, seventy-seven men.

15 And he came to the men of Sukkot and said, Behold Zevakh and Tzalmunna, about whom you taunted me, saying, Are the hands of Zevakh and Tzalmunna already in your hand, that we should give bread to your men who are exhausted?

16 And he took the elders of the city, and he took thorns of the wilderness and briers, and with them he taught the men of Sukkot a lesson.

17 And he broke down the tower of Penu'el and killed the men of the city.

18 Then he said to Zevakh and Tzalmunna, Where are the men whom you killed at Tavor? They answered, As you are, so were they. Every one of them resembled the son of a king.

19 And he said, They were my brothers, the sons of my mother. As YHWH lives, if you had saved them alive, I would not kill you.

20 So he said to Yeter his firstborn, Rise and kill them. But the young man did not draw his sword, for he was afraid, because he was still a young man.

21 Then Zevakh and Tzalmunna said, Rise yourself and fall upon us, for as the man is, so is his strength. And Gid'on arose and killed Zevakh and Tzalmunna, and he took the crescent ornaments that were on the necks of their camels.

22 Then the men of Yisra'EL said to Gid'on, Rule over us, you and your son and your grandson also, for you have saved us from the hand of Midyan.

23 Gid'on said to them, I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you — YHWH will rule over you.

24 And Gid'on said to them, Let me make a request of you: every one of you give me the earrings from his spoil. For they had golden earrings, because they were Ishmaelites.

25 And they answered, We will willingly give them. And they spread a cloak, and every man threw in it the earrings of his spoil.

26 And the weight of the golden earrings that he requested was seventeen hundred shekels of gold, besides the crescent ornaments and the pendants and the purple garments worn by the kings of Midyan, and besides the collars that were around the necks of their camels.

27 And Gid'on made an ephod of it and put it in his city, in Ofrah. And all Yisra'EL whored after it there, and it became a snare to Gid'on and to his family.

28 So Midyan was subdued before the people of Yisra'EL, and they raised their heads no more. And the land had rest forty years in the days of Gid'on.

29 Yeruba'al the son of Yo'ash went and lived in his own house.

30 Now Gid'on had seventy sons, his own offspring, for he had many wives.

31 And his concubine who was in Shekhem also bore him a son, and he called his name Avimэlekh.

32 And Gid'on the son of Yo'ash died in a good old age and was buried in the tomb of Yo'ash his father, at Ofrah of the Aviezrites.

33 As soon as Gid'on died, the people of Yisra'EL turned again and whored after the Ba'als and made Ba'al-Berit their god.

34 And the people of Yisra'EL did not remember YHWH their Creator, who had delivered them from the hand of all their enemies on every side,

35 And they did not show steadfast love to the family of Yeruba'al — that is, Gid'on — in return for all the good that he had done to Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 8. The chapter traces Gid'on's trajectory from covenant faithfulness to covenant failure. Verse 23 — I will not rule over you — YHWH will rule over you — is one of the clearest covenant political declarations in Shofetim, yet it is immediately followed by the ephod that becomes a snare.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 27 — Gid'on made an ephod of it and put it in his city, and all Yisra'EL whored after it there, and it became a snare to Gid'on and to his family — is read as the paradigm of covenant corruption: the instrument of victory becomes the instrument of idolatry.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 8. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The ephod made by Gid'on — verse 27 — mirrors the golden calf of Shemot 32: a religious object created from the spoil of victory that draws the covenant people into whoring after it.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 23 — YHWH will rule over you — is the covenant high point of Gid'on's career. He refuses the monarchy with a theologically precise answer. Yet within eight verses — verse 27 — he creates an ephod that becomes a snare to him and his family. The same man who declared YHWH's kingship created a religious object that displaced it. The capacity for covenant faithfulness and covenant failure coexist in the same person in the same lifetime. Gid'on dies in good old age — verse 32 — but verse 35 is his epitaph: the people did not show steadfast love to his family. The covenant memory lasts no longer than the judge.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 9 Avimelekh — The Parable of the Trees — Seventy Brothers on One Stone

1 Now Avimelekh the son of Yeruba'al went to Shekhem to his mother's brothers and said to them and to the whole clan of his mother's father,

2 Say in the ears of all the leaders of Shekhem, Which is better for you, that all seventy of the sons of Yeruba'al rule over you, or that one rule over you? Remember also that I am your bone and your flesh.

3 And his mother's brothers spoke all these words on his behalf in the ears of all the leaders of Shekhem, and their hearts inclined to follow Avimelekh, for they said, He is our brother.

4 And they gave him seventy pieces of silver out of the house of Ba'al-Berit with which Avimelekh hired worthless and reckless fellows, who followed him.

5 And he went to his father's house at Ofrah and killed his brothers the sons of Yeruba'al, seventy men, on one stone. But Yotam the youngest son of Yeruba'al was left, for he hid himself.

6 And all the leaders of Shekhem and all Beit-Milo came together, and they went and made Avimelekh king, by the oak of the pillar at Shekhem.

7 When it was told to Yotam, he went and stood on top of Mount Gerizim and cried aloud and said to them, Listen to me, you leaders of Shekhem, that Creator may listen to you.

8 The trees once went out to anoint a king over them, and they said to the olive tree, Reign over us.

9 But the olive tree said to them, Shall I leave my abundance, by which gods and men are honored, and go hold sway over the trees?

10 And the trees said to the fig tree, You come and reign over us.

11 But the fig tree said to them, Shall I leave my sweetness and my good fruit and go hold sway over the trees?

12 And the trees said to the vine, You come and reign over us.

13 But the vine said to them, Shall I leave my wine that cheers creators and men and go hold sway over the trees?

14 Then all the trees said to the bramble, You come and reign over us.

15 And the bramble said to the trees, If in good faith you are anointing me king over you, then come and take refuge in my shade, but if not, let fire come out of the bramble and devour the cedars of Lebanon.

16 Now therefore, if you acted in good faith and integrity when you made Avimelekh king, and if you have dealt well with Yeruba'al and his house and have done to him as his deeds deserved —

17 For my father fought for you and risked his life and delivered you from the hand of Midyan,

18 And you have risen up against my father's house this day and have killed his sons, seventy men on one stone, and have made Avimelekh, the son of his female servant, king over the leaders of Shekhem, because he is your relative —

19 If you then have acted in good faith and integrity with Yeruba'al and with his house this day, then rejoice in Avimelekh, and let him also rejoice in you.

20 But if not, let fire come out from Avimelekh and devour the leaders of Shekhem and Beit-Milo, and let fire come out from the leaders of Shekhem and from Beit-Milo and devour Avimelekh.

21 And Yotam ran away and fled and went to Be'er and lived there, because of Avimelekh his brother.

22 Avimelekh ruled over Yisra'EL three years.

23 And Creator sent an evil spirit between Avimelekh and the leaders of Shekhem, and the leaders of Shekhem dealt treacherously with Avimelekh,

24 That the violence done to the seventy sons of Yeruba'al might come, and their blood be laid on Avimelekh their brother, who killed them, and on the men of Shekhem, who strengthened his hands to kill his brothers.

25 And the leaders of Shekhem put men in ambush against him on the mountaintops, and they robbed all who passed by them along that way. And it was told to Avimelekh.

26 And Ga'al the son of Eved moved into Shekhem with his relatives, and the leaders of Shekhem put their trust in him.

27 And they went out into the field and gathered the grapes from their vineyards and trod them and held a festival and went into the house of their god and ate and drank and reviled Avimelekh.

28 And Ga'al the son of Eved said, Who is Avimelekh, and who are we of Shekhem, that we should serve him? Is he not the son of Yeruba'al, and is not Zevul his officer? Serve the men of Khamor the father of Shekhem, but why should we serve him?

29 Would that this people were under my hand. Then I would remove Avimelekh. I would say to Avimelekh, Increase your army, and come out.

30 When Zevul the ruler of the city heard the words of Ga'al the son of Eved, his anger was kindled.

31 And he sent messengers to Avimelekh secretly, saying, Behold, Ga'al the son of Eved and his relatives have come to Shekhem, and they are stirring up the city against you.

32 Now therefore, go by night, you and the people who are with you, and set an ambush in the field.

33 Then in the morning, as soon as the sun is up, rise early and rush upon the city. And when he and the people who are with him come out against you, you may do to them as your hand finds to do.

34 So Avimelekh and all the men who were with him rose up by night and set an ambush against Shekhem in four companies.

35 And Ga'al the son of Eved went out and stood in the entrance of the gate of the city, and Avimelekh and the people who were with him rose from the ambush.

36 And when Ga'al saw the people, he said to Zevul, Behold, people are coming down from the mountaintops. And Zevul said to him, You mistake the shadow of the mountains for men.

37 Ga'al spoke again and said, Behold, people are coming down from the center of the land, and one company is coming from the direction of the Diviners' Oak.

38 Then Zevul said to him, Where is your mouth now, you who said, Who is Avimelekh, that we should serve him? Are not these the people whom you despised? Go out now and fight with them.

39 And Ga'al went out before the leaders of Shekhem and fought with Avimelekh.

40 And Avimelekh chased him, and he fled before him. And many fell wounded, up to the entrance of the gate.

41 And Avimelekh lived at Arumah, and Zevul drove out Ga'al and his relatives, so that they could not dwell at Shekhem.

42 On the following day, the people went out into the field, and it was told to Avimelekh.

43 He took his people and divided them into three companies and set an ambush in the fields. And he looked and saw the people coming out of the city. So he rose against them and killed them.

44 Avimelekh and the company that was with him rushed forward and stood at the entrance of the gate of the city, while the two companies rushed upon all who were in the field and killed them.

45 And Avimelekh fought against the city all that day. He captured the city and killed the people who were in it, and he razed the city and sowed it with salt.

46 When all the leaders of the Tower of Shekhem heard of it, they entered the stronghold of the house of El-Berit.

47 Avimelekh was told that all the leaders of the Tower of Shekhem were gathered together.

48 And Avimelekh went up to Mount Tzalmon, he and all the people who were with him. And Avimelekh took an axe in his hand and cut down a bundle of brushwood and took it up and laid it on his shoulder. And he said to the people who were with him, What you have seen me do, hurry and do as I have done.

49 So every one of the people cut down his bundle and following Avimelekh put it against the stronghold, and they set the stronghold on fire over them, so that all the people of the Tower of Shekhem also died, about a thousand men and women.

50 Then Avimelekh went to Tevetz and encamped against Tevetz and captured it.

51 But there was a strong tower within the city, and all the men and women and all the leaders of the city fled to it and shut themselves in, and they went up to the roof of the tower.

52 And Avimelekh came to the tower and fought against it and drew near to the door of the tower to burn it with fire.

53 And a certain woman threw an upper millstone on Avimelekh's head and crushed his skull.

54 Then he called quickly to the young man his armor-bearer and said to him, Draw your sword and kill me, lest they say of me, A woman killed him. And his young man thrust him through, and he died.

55 And when the men of Yisra'EL saw that Avimelekh was dead, everyone departed to his home.

56 Thus Creator returned the evil of Avimelekh, which he committed against his father in killing his seventy brothers.

57 And Creator also made all the evil of the men of Shekhem return on their heads, and upon them came the curse of Yotam the son of Yeruba'al.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 9. The Avimelekh narrative is the longest chapter in Shofetim. Yotam's parable of the trees — verses 8-15 — is the first extended political parable in the Nevi'im and a devastating critique of illegitimate monarchy: only the bramble, which is useless for everything else, is willing to be king.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 56-57 — Creator returned the evil of Avimelekh and made the evil of the men of Shekhem return on their heads — is read as the covenant declaration of moral reciprocity: YHWH does not prevent the consequences of covenant violence but directs them back to their source.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 9. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The woman who kills Avimelekh with a millstone — verse 53 — is the second unnamed woman in Shofetim who defeats a military leader, echoing Ya'el's killing of Sisera in Chapter 4.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Yotam's parable — verses 8-15 — is political theology in miniature. The productive trees — olive, fig, vine — refuse kingship because it would require them to abandon their productive purpose. Only the bramble, which produces nothing useful, accepts the kingship and immediately threatens the cedars of Lebanon with fire. The parable's point: those who seek power most aggressively are precisely those least qualified to hold it — they produce nothing, and their power serves only their own inflation. Verse 53-54 — a woman threw a millstone on Avimelekh's head — and he said to his armor-bearer, draw your sword and kill me lest they say, a woman killed him. Avimelekh's final concern is reputation. He dies on his own terms — or thinks he does. Creator had already determined the outcome — verse 56.

YIFTAKH — CHAPTERS 10 THROUGH 12

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 10 Tola and Ya'ir — Yisra'EL Cries Out Again — We Have Sinned Against You

1 After Avimelekh there arose to save Yisra'EL Tola the son of Pu'ah, son of Dodo, a man of Yissaskhar, and he lived at Shamir in the hill country of Efrayim.

2 And he judged Yisra'EL twenty-three years. Then he died and was buried at Shamir.

3 After him arose Ya'ir the Gil'adite, who judged Yisra'EL twenty-two years.

4 And he had thirty sons who rode on thirty donkeys, and they had thirty cities, called Khayyot-Ya'ir to this day, which are in the land of Gil'ad.

5 And Ya'ir died and was buried in Kamon.

6 The people of Yisra'EL again did what was evil in the sight of YHWH and served the Ba'als and the Ashtarots, the gods of Aram, the gods of Tzidon, the gods of Moav, the gods of the Ammonites, and the gods of the Pelishtim. And they forsook YHWH and did not serve him.

7 So the anger of YHWH was kindled against Yisra'EL, and he sold them into the hand of the Pelishtim and into the hand of the Ammonites,

8 And they crushed and oppressed the people of Yisra'EL that year. For eighteen years they oppressed all the people of Yisra'EL who were beyond the Yarden in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gil'ad.

9 And the Ammonites crossed the Yarden to fight also against Yehudah and against Binyamin and against the house of Efrayim, so that Yisra'EL was severely distressed.

10 And the people of Yisra'EL cried out to YHWH, saying, We have sinned against you, because we have forsaken our Creator and have served the Ba'als.

11 And YHWH said to the people of Yisra'EL, Did I not save you from the Egyptians and from the Amorites, from the Ammonites and from the Pelishtim?

12 The Tzidonians also, and the Amalekites and the Maonites oppressed you, and you cried out to me, and I saved you out of their hand.

13 Yet you have forsaken me and served other gods — therefore I will save you no more.

14 Go and cry out to the gods whom you have chosen — let them save you in the time of your distress.

15 And the people of Yisra'EL said to YHWH, We have sinned — do to us whatever seems good to you. Only please deliver us this day.

16 So they put away the foreign gods from among them and served YHWH, and he became impatient over the misery of Yisra'EL.

17 Then the Ammonites were called to arms, and they encamped in Gil'ad. And the people of Yisra'EL came together and encamped at Mitzpah.

18 And the people, the leaders of Gil'ad, said one to another, Who is the man who will begin to fight against the Ammonites? He shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gil'ad.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 10. The brief notices of Tola and Ya'ir — verses 1-5 — are two of the so-called minor judges. Verse 13-14 — YHWH's refusal to save — is the most severe covenant response to Yisra'EL's repeated apostasy in the entire book. YHWH tells them to cry out to the gods they have chosen.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 16 — he became impatient over the misery of Yisra'EL — the Hebrew tiktzar nafsho — his soul was shortened, or he could endure it no more — is read as one of the most remarkable covenant statements in the Nevi'im: YHWH's compassion overrides his declared refusal to save.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 10. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 16 — YHWH became impatient over the misery of Yisra'EL — is one of the most theologically dense verses in Shofetim: after declaring he will save them no more, YHWH is moved by their suffering.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13-16 — the most theologically compressed exchange in Shofetim. YHWH declares he will no longer save them — go cry to your chosen gods. Yisra'EL confesses and puts away the foreign gods. YHWH becomes impatient over their misery and saves them. The sequence reveals the covenant character of YHWH with precision: his declarations of judgment are not mechanical pronouncements but covenant warnings. When the covenant people genuinely repent — not merely verbally but by putting away the foreign gods — verse 16 — YHWH's covenant compassion overrides the covenant warning. His mercy is not weakness but the deepest expression of covenant faithfulness.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 11 Yiftakh the Gil'adite — His Vow to YHWH — His Daughter

1 Now Yiftakh the Gil'adite was a mighty warrior, but he was the son of a prostitute. Gil'ad was the father of Yiftakh.

2 And Gil'ad's wife also bore him sons. And when his wife's sons grew up, they drove Yiftakh out and said to him, You shall not have an inheritance in our father's house, for you are the son of another woman.

3 Then Yiftakh fled from his brothers and lived in the land of Tov, and worthless fellows collected around Yiftakh and went out with him.

4 After a time the Ammonites made war against Yisra'EL.

5 And when the Ammonites made war against Yisra'EL, the elders of Gil'ad went to bring Yiftakh from the land of Tov.

6 And they said to Yiftakh, Come and be our leader, that we may fight against the Ammonites.

7 But Yiftakh said to the elders of Gil'ad, Did you not hate me and drive me out of my father's house? Why have you come to me now when you are in distress?

8 And the elders of Gil'ad said to Yiftakh, That is why we have turned to you now, that you may go with us and fight against the Ammonites and be our head over all the inhabitants of Gil'ad.

9 Yiftakh said to the elders of Gil'ad, If you bring me home again to fight against the Ammonites, and YHWH gives them over to me, I will be your head.

10 And the elders of Gil'ad said to Yiftakh, YHWH will be witness between us, if we do not do as you say.

11 So Yiftakh went with the elders of Gil'ad, and the people made him head and leader over them. And Yiftakh spoke all his words before YHWH at Mitzpah.

12 And Yiftakh sent messengers to the king of the Ammonites and said, What do you have against me, that you have come to me to fight against my land?

13 And the king of the Ammonites answered the messengers of Yiftakh, Because Yisra'EL on coming up from Egypt took away my land, from the Arnon to the Yabbok and to the Yarden. Now therefore restore it peaceably.

14 Yiftakh again sent messengers to the king of the Ammonites

15 And said to him, Thus says Yiftakh: Yisra'EL did not take away the land of Moav or the land of the Ammonites,

16 But when they came up from Egypt, Yisra'EL went through the wilderness to the Red Sea and came to Kadesh.

17 Yisra'EL then sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying, Please let me pass through your land, but the king of Edom would not listen. And they also sent to the king of Moav, but he would not consent. So Yisra'EL remained at Kadesh.

18 Then they journeyed through the wilderness and went around the land of Edom and the land of Moav and arrived on the east side of the land of Moav and camped on the other side of the Arnon. But they did not enter the territory of Moav, for the Arnon was the boundary of Moav.

19 Yisra'EL then sent messengers to Sikhon king of the Amorites, king of Kheshbon, and Yisra'EL said to him, Please let us pass through your land to our country.

20 But Sikhon did not trust Yisra'EL to pass through his territory, so Sikhon gathered all his people together and encamped at Yahatz and fought with Yisra'EL.

21 And YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, gave Sikhon and all his people into the hand of Yisra'EL, and they defeated them. So Yisra'EL took possession of all the land of the Amorites, who inhabited that country.

22 And they took possession of all the territory of the Amorites from the Arnon to the Yabbok and from the wilderness to the Yarden.

23 So then YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, dispossessed the Amorites from before his people Yisra'EL, and are you to take possession of them?

24 Will you not possess what Kemosh your god gives you to possess? And all that YHWH our Creator has dispossessed before us, we will possess.

25 And now are you any better than Balak the son of Tzippor, king of Moav? Did he ever contend against Yisra'EL, or did he ever go to war with them?

26 While Yisra'EL lived in Kheshbon and its villages, and in Aro'er and its villages, and in all the cities that are on the banks of the Arnon, three hundred years, why did you not deliver them within that time?

27 I therefore have not sinned against you, and you do me wrong by making war on me. May YHWH, the Judge, decide this day between the people of Yisra'EL and the people of Ammon.

28 But the king of the Ammonites did not listen to the words of Yiftakh that he sent to him.

29 Then the Spirit of YHWH was upon Yiftakh, and he passed through Gil'ad and Menasheh and passed on to Mitzpah of Gil'ad, and from Mitzpah of Gil'ad he passed on to the Ammonites.

30 And Yiftakh made a vow to YHWH and said, If you will give the Ammonites into my hand,

31 Then whatever comes out from the doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from the Ammonites shall be YHWH's, and I will offer it up for a burnt offering.

32 So Yiftakh crossed over to the Ammonites to fight against them, and YHWH gave them into his hand.

33 And he struck them from Aro'er to the neighborhood of Minnit, twenty cities, and as far as Avel-Keramim, with a great blow. So the Ammonites were subdued before the people of Yisra'EL.

34 Then Yiftakh came to his home at Mitzpah. And behold, his daughter came out to meet him with tambourines and with dances. She was his only child — beside her he had neither son nor daughter.

35 And as soon as he saw her, he tore his clothes and said, Alas, my daughter. You have brought me very low, and you have become the cause of great trouble to me. For I have opened my mouth to YHWH, and I cannot take back my vow.

36 And she said to him, My father, you have opened your mouth to YHWH — do to me according to what has gone out of your mouth, now that YHWH has avenged you on your enemies, on the Ammonites.

37 So she said to her father, Let this thing be done for me: leave me alone two months, that I may go up and down on the mountains and weep for my virginity, I and my companions.

38 So he said, Go. Then he sent her away for two months, and she departed, she and her companions, and wept for her virginity on the mountains.

39 And at the end of two months, she returned to her father, who did with her according to his vow that he had made. She had never known a man, and it became a custom in Yisra'EL

40 That the daughters of Yisra'EL went year by year to lament the daughter of Yiftakh the Gil'adite four days in the year.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 11. Yiftakh's covenant negotiation with the Ammonite king — verses 12-27 — is the most extended diplomatic exchange in Shofetim, grounding Yisra'EL's territorial claim in the historical record of the wilderness journey. The Spirit of YHWH comes upon Yiftakh before the vow — verse 29 — meaning the victory was already guaranteed when the vow was made.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full. The daughter of Yiftakh is unnamed in the text but is commemorated annually — verse 40. The Beta Israel tradition reads her response in verse 36 as the highest expression of covenant submission: she accepts the consequence of her father's vow without resistance, asking only for two months to mourn her virginity.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 11. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The annual lamentation of the daughters of Yisra'EL for Yiftakh's daughter — verse 40 — is one of the few references to a women's liturgical practice preserved in the Nevi'im.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 29-31 — the Spirit of YHWH was upon Yiftakh and then he made his vow. The sequence is critical: YHWH had already come upon Yiftakh before the vow was made. The victory was YHWH's gift — the vow added nothing to the outcome. The tragedy of Yiftakh's daughter is therefore not the price of victory but the unnecessary consequence of a rash covenant oath. Verse 35 — you have become the cause of great trouble to me. Yiftakh's response to seeing his daughter is to blame her. She did nothing wrong. His vow was rash. His grief is real — but his framing of her as the cause is a further failure of covenant character.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 12 The Efrayimites and the Shibboleth — Three Minor Judges

1 The men of Efrayim were called to arms, and they crossed to Tzafon and said to Yiftakh, Why did you cross over to fight against the Ammonites and did not call us to go with you? We will burn your house over you with fire.

2 And Yiftakh said to them, I and my people had a great dispute with the Ammonites, and when I called you, you did not save me from their hand.

3 And when I saw that you would not save me, I took my life in my hand and crossed over against the Ammonites, and YHWH gave them into my hand. Why then have you come up to me this day to fight against me?

4 Then Yiftakh gathered all the men of Gil'ad and fought with Efrayim. And the men of Gil'ad struck Efrayim, because they said, You are fugitives of Efrayim, you Gil'adites, in the midst of Efrayim and Menasheh.

5 And the Gil'adites captured the fords of the Yarden against the Efrayimites. And when any of the fugitives of Efrayim said, Let me go over, the men of Gil'ad said to him, Are you an Efrayimite? When he said, No,

6 They said to him, Then say Shibboleth, and he said Sibboleth, for he could not pronounce it right. Then they seized him and slaughtered him at the fords of the Yarden. At that time forty-two thousand of the Efrayimites fell.

7 Yiftakh judged Yisra'EL six years. Then Yiftakh the Gil'adite died and was buried in his city in Gil'ad.

8 After him Ivtzan of Beit-Lekhem judged Yisra'EL.

9 He had thirty sons, and thirty daughters he gave in marriage outside his clan, and thirty daughters he brought in from outside for his sons. And he judged Yisra'EL seven years.

10 Then Ivtzan died and was buried at Beit-Lekhem.

11 After him Elon the Zevulunite judged Yisra'EL, and he judged Yisra'EL ten years.

12 Then Elon the Zevulunite died and was buried at Ayalon in the land of Zevulun.

13 After him Avdon the son of Hillel the Pirathanite judged Yisra'EL.

14 He had forty sons and thirty grandsons, who rode on seventy donkeys, and he judged Yisra'EL eight years.

15 Then Avdon the son of Hillel the Pirathanite died and was buried at Piraton in the land of Efrayim, in the hill country of the Amalekites.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 12. The Shibboleth test — verse 6 — is one of the most linguistically significant passages in the Hebrew canon: the dialectal difference between Efrayimite and Gil'adite pronunciation of the same word — shin versus sin — used as a lethal identification test.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. The intertribal violence of the Shibboleth episode — forty-two thousand Efrayimites killed by fellow Yisra'ELites — is read as the covenant consequence of the pride and exclusivism that drove the Efrayimites' complaint against both Gid'on in Chapter 8 and Yiftakh here.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 12. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The three minor judges — Ivtzan, Elon, Avdon — are described only by their family size and their years of service, providing no narrative detail.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — say Shibboleth — he said Sibboleth, for he could not pronounce it right — then they slaughtered him. The word shibboleth — meaning ear of grain or flowing stream — became the test of identity and the mechanism of death. A single phoneme determined life or death. The covenant community, instead of fighting the Ammonites together, turns its weapons on itself. The intertribal violence of Chapter 12 is the direct consequence of the pride of Efrayim — the same pride that drove their complaint against Gid'on in Chapter 8:1. Efrayim's sense of covenant privilege becomes the source of covenant self-destruction.

SHIMSHON — CHAPTERS 13 THROUGH 14

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 13 The Birth of Shimshon — The Angel of YHWH Appears to Manoakh and His Wife — His Name Is Wonderful

1 And the people of Yisra'EL again did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, so YHWH gave them into the hand of the Pelishtim for forty years.

2 There was a certain man of Tzorah, of the tribe of the Danites, whose name was Manoakh. And his wife was barren and had no children.

3 And the angel of YHWH appeared to the woman and said to her, Behold, you are barren and have not borne children, but you shall conceive and bear a son.

4 Therefore be careful and drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean,

5 For behold, you shall conceive and bear a son. No razor shall come upon his head, for the child shall be a Nazirite to Creator from the womb, and he shall begin to save Yisra'EL from the hand of the Pelishtim.

6 Then the woman came and told her husband, A man of Creator came to me, and his appearance was like the appearance of the angel of Creator, very awesome. I did not ask him where he was from, and he did not tell me his name.

7 But he said to me, Behold, you shall conceive and bear a son. So then drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean, for the child shall be a Nazirite to Creator from the womb to the day of his death.

8 Then Manoakh prayed to YHWH and said, O Lord, please let the man of Creator whom you sent come again to us and teach us what we are to do with the child who will be born.

9 And Creator listened to the voice of Manoakh, and the angel of Creator came again to the woman as she sat in the field. But Manoakh her husband was not with her.

10 So the woman ran quickly and told her husband, Behold, the man who came to me the other day has appeared to me.

11 And Manoakh arose and went after his wife and came to the man and said to him, Are you the man who spoke to this woman? And he said, I am.

12 And Manoakh said, Now when your words come true, what is to be the child's manner of life, and what is his mission?

13 And the angel of YHWH said to Manoakh, Of all that I said to the woman let her be careful.

14 She may not eat of anything that comes from the vine, neither let her drink wine or strong drink, or eat any unclean thing. All that I commanded her let her observe.

15 Manoakh said to the angel of YHWH, Please let us detain you and prepare a young goat for you.

16 And the angel of YHWH said to Manoakh, If you detain me, I will not eat of your food. But if you prepare a burnt offering, then offer it to YHWH. For Manoakh did not know that he was the angel of YHWH.

17 And Manoakh said to the angel of YHWH, What is your name, so that, when your words come true, we may honor you?

18 And the angel of YHWH said to him, Why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful?

19 So Manoakh took the young goat with the grain offering, and offered it on the rock to YHWH, to the one who works wonders, and Manoakh and his wife were watching.

20 And when the flame went up toward heaven from the altar, the angel of YHWH went up in the flame of the altar. Now Manoakh and his wife were watching, and they fell on their faces to the ground.

21 The angel of YHWH appeared no more to Manoakh and to his wife. Then Manoakh knew that he was the angel of YHWH.

22 And Manoakh said to his wife, We shall surely die, for we have seen Creator.

23 But his wife said to him, If YHWH had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted a burnt offering and a grain offering at our hands, or shown us all these things, or now announced to us such things as these.

24 And the woman bore a son and called his name Shimshon. And the young man grew, and YHWH blessed him.

25 And the Spirit of YHWH began to stir him in Makhaneh-Dan, between Tzorah and Eshtaol.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 13. The birth announcement to Manoakh's unnamed wife follows the covenant pattern of barren women whose sons become covenant instruments: Sarah, Rivkah, Rakhel, Khannah. The Nazirite vow imposed from birth makes Shimshon unique — he does not choose the vow, it is chosen for him before conception.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 13 in full. Manoakh's wife — unnamed throughout — is the more theologically perceptive of the two parents. Her reasoning in verse 23 — if YHWH meant to kill us he would not have accepted our offering — demonstrates covenant theological reasoning of a high order.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 13. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 18 — why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful — the Hebrew peli — wonderful, set apart, incomprehensible — is the same root as the name Pele in the covenant blessing traditions.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18 — why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful? The angel of YHWH's name is beyond human comprehension — it is peli — wonderful, incomprehensible. The same theology as YHWH's self-disclosure to Moshe in Shemot 3: the name is given but exceeds the capacity of the name to contain it. Verse 23 — Manoakh's wife demonstrates superior covenant reasoning to her husband. She does not panic. She reasons from what YHWH has already done — accepted the offering, shown them these things, announced these words — to what he will therefore not do. YHWH's past faithfulness is the ground of covenant confidence. She is a model of covenant reasoning under existential pressure.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 14 Shimshon's Marriage at Timnah — Out of the Eater Something to Eat — The Riddle

1 Shimshon went down to Timnah, and at Timnah he saw one of the daughters of the Pelishtim.

2 Then he came up and told his father and mother, I saw one of the daughters of the Pelishtim at Timnah. Now get her for me as my wife.

3 But his father and mother said to him, Is there not a woman among the daughters of your relatives, or among all our people, that you must go to take a wife from the uncircumcised Pelishtim? But Shimshon said to his father, Get her for me, for she is right in my eyes.

4 His father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH, for he was seeking an opportunity against the Pelishtim. At that time the Pelishtim ruled over Yisra'EL.

5 Then Shimshon went down with his father and mother to Timnah, and they came to the vineyards of Timnah. And behold, a young lion came toward him roaring.

6 Then the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he tore the lion in pieces as one tears a young goat. He had nothing in his hand. But he did not tell his father or his mother what he had done.

7 Then he went down and talked with the woman, and she was right in Shimshon's eyes.

8 After some days he returned to take her. And he turned aside to see the carcass of the lion, and behold, there was a swarm of bees in the body of the lion, and honey.

9 He scraped it out into his hands and went on, eating as he went. And he came to his father and mother and gave some to them, and they ate. But he did not tell them that he had scraped the honey from the carcass of the lion.

10 His father went down to the woman, and Shimshon prepared a feast there, for so the young men used to do.

11 As soon as the people saw him, they brought thirty companions to be with him.

12 And Shimshon said to them, Let me now put a riddle to you. If you can tell me what it is, within the seven days of the feast, and find it out, then I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes,

13 But if you cannot tell me what it is, then you shall give me thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes. And they said to him, Put your riddle, that we may hear it.

14 And he said to them, Out of the eater came something to eat. Out of the strong came something sweet. And in three days they could not solve the riddle.

15 On the fourth day they said to Shimshon's wife, Entice your husband to tell us what the riddle is, lest we burn you and your father's house with fire. Have you invited us here to impoverish us?

16 And Shimshon's wife wept over him and said, You only hate me — you do not love me. You have put a riddle to my people, and you have not told me what it is. And he said to her, Behold, I have not told my father nor my mother, and shall I tell you?

17 She wept before him the seven days that their feast lasted, and on the seventh day he told her, because she pressed him hard. Then she told the riddle to her people.

18 And the men of the city said to him on the seventh day before the sun went down, What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion? And he said to them, If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have found out my riddle.

19 And the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon and struck down thirty men of the town and took their spoil and gave the changes of clothes to those who had told the riddle. In hot anger he went back to his father's house.

20 And Shimshon's wife was given to his companion, who had been his best man.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 14. The Shimshon narrative — Chapters 13-16 — is the longest individual judge narrative in Shofetim. Verse 4 — it was from YHWH, for he was seeking an opportunity against the Pelishtim — is the theological key to the entire Shimshon account: YHWH works through Shimshon's personal impulses to accomplish covenant purposes.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 14 in full. The riddle — out of the eater came something to eat, out of the strong came something sweet — is read as the covenant paradox at the heart of Shimshon's entire life: his greatest strength becomes the source of his deepest vulnerability.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 14. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The Spirit of YHWH rushing upon Shimshon appears in verse 6 and verse 19 — both times in connection with acts of lethal violence. The Spirit's activity in Shimshon is not sanctifying but empowering — equipping him for the specific covenant task of afflicting the Pelishtim.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — it was from YHWH, for he was seeking an opportunity against the Pelishtim. The narrator reveals what no character in the story knows: YHWH is directing events through Shimshon's personal desires. Shimshon is not acting in covenant faithfulness — he wants a Pelishti woman because she is right in his eyes — verse 3. Yet YHWH works through this morally ambiguous choice to accomplish his covenant purpose. The Shimshon narratives consistently present a judge whose personal failures YHWH uses rather than prevents. This is covenant providence operating through human brokenness rather than through covenant faithfulness.

END OF PART TWO — CHAPTERS 8 THROUGH 14
